params.text:
Other problems to watch for

As well as infection, several other problems can affect a catheter, and each one needs reporting. If no urine drains for a few hours, or the person has tummy pain and feels their bladder is full, the catheter may be blocked or kinked. Bypassing is when urine leaks around the outside of the catheter, which can be a sign of a blockage, a bladder spasm or a problem with the catheter itself.

  • Not draining: a possible blockage or a kinked tube.
  • Bypassing: urine leaking around the catheter.
  • Blood or pain: visible blood, or new pain and discomfort.

Blood, pain and a catheter that comes out

Always report blood in the urine, clots, or heavily bloodstained urine, as well as any new pain, discomfort or dragging sensation. If the catheter falls out or is pulled out, this is not something you replace — only a trained clinician can do that. Reassure the person, keep them comfortable and clean, note the time it happened, and contact the nurse straight away, because a person who normally cannot pass urine may be unable to empty their bladder without it.

The rule with every problem is the same: you observe and report, you do not attempt to fix, unblock, reinsert or flush the catheter yourself. First check simple, safe things within your role — that the person is not lying on the tube, that it is not kinked, and that the bag is below the bladder — then escalate anything you cannot resolve to the registered nurse.

params.question: A person's catheter has fallen out. What should you do?

answers.text: Reassure the person, note the time, keep them comfortable, and contact the registered nurse straight away.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. You never reinsert a catheter; report it promptly so a trained clinician can act.

answers.text: Gently push it back in yourself to save time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — you must never reinsert a catheter; only a trained clinician can do that.

answers.text: Do nothing, as a catheter falling out is never a problem.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a person who cannot pass urine may be unable to empty their bladder, so report it at once.

answers.text: Flush the catheter with water before replacing it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — you never flush or reinsert a catheter; escalate to the nurse.
